item.title: Seized ship taken toward Iran as Trump and Xi agree on Strait of Hormuz
item.description: A ship was taken by unknown parties toward Iranian waters after an Indian-flagged vessel was attacked off Oman.

item.title: Why are so many U.S. CEOs in China with Trump, and what do they want?
item.description: More than a dozen American CEOs are accompanying President Trump on his trip to China. That's not unusual.

item.title: Why a $10,000 CD account is worth opening this May
item.description: Have $10,000 in your savings account now? Here are three reasons why you should move into a CD account this month.

item.title: Breaking down the U.S. "learning recession" found in new Education Scorecard report
item.description: A new report from the national Education Scorecard warns the U.S. is experiencing a "learning recession." Thomas Kane, professor at Harvard's Center for Education Policy Research, joins CBS News to discuss.

item.title: 3 credit card debt questions borrowers should consider this week
item.description: Following a series of disappointing inflation reports, credit card users in debt should consider these questions now.

item.title: Former death row inmate granted bond, after nearly being executed 3 times
item.description: An Oklahoma judge granted bond to former death row inmate Richard Glossip on Thursday, laying the groundwork for his first release from prison since 1997.

item.title: Crowdstrike counter adversary chief on cyber threats from China, North Korea
item.description: Adam Meyers, head of counter adversary operations for Crowdstrike, joins CBS News to discuss how Americans should view potential cyber threats from China and North Korea.

item.title: CENTCOM chief says Iran's hold on strait has weakened, but threats remain
item.description: Admiral Brad Cooper of U.S. Central Command told the Senate Armed Services Committee that U.S. forces have destroyed more than 90% of Iran's inventory of 8,000 naval mines.

item.title: House leaders to take action on process for sexual misconduct claims
item.description: Speaker Mike Johnson and Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries are launching a bipartisan task force aimed at addressing how sexual misconduct claims are handled within the House of Representatives, multiple sources confirm to CBS News.

item.title: Ukraine says Russia launches "virtually nonstop" strikes on Kyiv
item.description: More than 1,500 Russian drones and dozens of missiles were launched in the last two days, according to Ukrainian officials.

item.title: Bipartisan U.S.-Mexico congressional caucus launches amid diplomatic strain
item.description: The launch is being backed by the American Mexican Leadership Council, a new national organization also debuting Thursday to elevate Mexican American leadership and advance U.S.-Mexico collaboration.

item.title: U.S. Border Patrol chief resigns in latest immigration shakeup
item.description: U.S. Border Patrol Chief Michael Banks, who was appointed to the role last year, told staff on Thursday that he is stepping down.

item.title: Closing arguments underway in Musk lawsuit against OpenAI
item.description: Closing arguments have begun in Elon Musk's $150 billion civil lawsuit against OpenAI CEO Sam Altman. CBS News correspondent Shanelle Kaul has more.

item.title: Spencer Pratt responds to claims he's been staying in luxury hotel, not trailer
item.description: Former reality TV star turned L.A. mayoral candidate Spencer Pratt is responding to reports that he's been staying at a luxury hotel in L.A. and not a trailer as he claimed. Pratt lost his home in the L.A. fires last year and in an interview with TMZ said he did spend around six days last month at a hotel in part due to security concerns.

item.title: Tech CEOs in China with Trump make cases for more economic partnerships
item.description: Several tech CEOs are with President Trump for the historic China summit with President Xi Jinping to advocate for more economic partnerships. CBS News' Nancy Cordes reports.

item.title: 4 things retirees should do immediately after receiving a garnishment notice
item.description: A garnishment notice can be alarming for retirees, but the right response could help protect your savings.

item.title: Software CEO convicted for running $1 billion Medicare fraud scheme
item.description: Brett Blackman was convicted on charges including healthcare and Medicare fraud, and faces decades in prison.

item.title: Senate adopts resolution to withhold senators' pay during shutdowns
item.description: The Senate unanimously agreed to adopt a resolution on Thursday that will withhold senators' pay during a lapse in funding for any federal agency.

item.title: What does China want in Taiwan?
item.description: Xi Jinping's ambitions for Taiwan are becoming a central topic of discussion during the Trump summit in China. Eyck Freymann, a Hoover Fellow at Stanford University, joins CBS News with more insight.

item.title: Ukrainians buried under rubble after deadly Russian strike
item.description: Rescue efforts continue in Kyiv, Ukraine, after two days of deadly Russian strikes. CBS News' Aidan Stretch reports.

item.title: Behind the rise of fast food and how it shaped the U.S.
item.description: In the series "USA to Z," "CBS Mornings" goes behind the scenes for a taste of history and shows how fast food has become part of American culture.

item.title: Body of second U.S. soldier who went missing in Morocco recovered
item.description: The Army identified the soldier as Spc. Mariyah Collington.

item.title: Extended interview: Martin Short
item.description: In this web exclusive, correspondent Tracy Smith sits down with comedian Martin Short, star of "SCTV," "Saturday Night Live" and "Only Murders in the Building," to talk about how director Lawrence Kasdan convinced him to become the subject of the documentary, "Martin: Life Is Short." They also discuss applying his childhood fantasies in show business; experiencing grief and loss as a young man; his first professional job on "Godspell" with Gilda Radner; and the value of reading his own reviews. He also describes his treasured characters like Ed Grimley, Nathan Thurm and Jiminy Glick.

item.title: Extended interview: Dan Rather on CBS News Radio
item.description: In this web exclusive, CBS News veteran Dan Rather talks with Mo Rocca about the impact that the heralded CBS Radio News had on him – as a child growing up in Texas, and as a young journalist learning by the example of "Murrow's Boys." He also discusses CBS Radio News' role in the evolution of broadcast journalism.

item.title: After growing up homeless, woman shares new apartment with her mom
item.description: Twenty-six-year-old Ana Duarte said that as a child, she and her mother were homeless in Florida. Now, she has found an apartment for her and her mom. Steve Hartman has the story.

item.title: Extended interview: Sting
item.description: In this web exclusive, the rock musician Sting talks with Mark Phillips about his stage musical, "The Last Ship," in which he stars, and which is being performed on a global tour. He calls the show an elegy for what Newcastle and its people represented to him growing up. He also discusses why, for him, uncertainty is a key component of art; why performing "Roxanne" today is never tiresome; and why, for him, music is a church.

item.title: Extended interview: Don Cheadle
item.description: Oscar-nominated actor Don Cheadle has appeared in more than 100 films and TV shows. But it had been a quarter-century since he appeared on stage in the Off-Broadway hit "Topdog/Underdog," until he made his Broadway debut this spring in a revival of "Proof." In this web exclusive, he talks with Tracy Smith about the lessons of his parents; catching the "theater bug" as a child; the "hamster wheel" of an actor's career; and his emotional investment in works like "Hotel Rwanda."

item.title: Dan Levy talks series "Big Mistakes" and reflects on "Schitt's Creek"
item.description: Dan Levy talks to "CBS Mornings" about the comedy series "Big Mistakes," which is about organized crime. Levy explains how he used his own life to help shape his character's relationships and reflects on the beloved series "Schitt's Creek."

item.title: Extended interview: Dan Levy
item.description: In this web exclusive, Dan Levy, the Emmy-winning co-creator and star of "Schitt's Creek," talks with Anthony Mason about his new Netflix series, "Big Mistakes," and how being "riddled with anxiety and self-doubt" was a driving force for his creative process.

item.title: "Schitt's Creek" star Dan Levy on making "Big Mistakes"
item.description: Dan Levy, the Emmy-winning co-creator of "Schitt's Creek," talks with Anthony Mason about his new sitcom, "Big Mistakes," the story of a New Jersey pastor and his sister (played by Taylor Ortega) who fall into a relationship with organized crime. He also discusses his early days as an MTV VJ; revisits Goodwood, Ontario (the small town where "Schitt's Creek" was filmed); and talks about the impact made by his co-stars, Catherine O'Hara and father Eugene Levy.
